00b
What Informed These Decisions

What's True About This Market

Industry context — key figures from public sources and Campspot's own data

$10.9BU.S. industry revenue, 2025IBISWorld 20267 · estimate
8.4%5-year CAGR 2020–2025Kentley Insights 20258 · estimate
3,500+Parks on Campspot across U.S. & CanadaCampspot.com · confirmed4
54MSite nights on Campspot in 2025Campspot 2025 Wrapped1
−2.2%Transient demand YoY (2026)CEO Webinar Jun 20266
+10.9%Glamping resorts YoY vs. −5.6% other parksCEO Webinar Jun 20266

Tailwinds

  • Glamping fastest-growing segment — weather-independent, experience-first
  • 70%+ of campers book online
  • Multi-property groups scaling — enterprise tools underserved
  • AI-assisted operations becoming viable

Headwinds

  • 2026 demand softening — gas +38% YoY, consumer confidence near record lows6
  • Southeast drought worst on record — −3.1% regional demand6
  • PE consolidation across both parks and software raising prices11,12
  • Marriott, Hilton, Hyatt all entered outdoor in 20249,10 — guest expectations raised permanently

What this means for the PMS

  • Operators running lean need software that works — not software that needs managing
  • The data moat (3.7M reservations, benchmarks, analytics) is defensible — but only if operators trust the platform enough to stay
  • Softer demand amplifies the cost of platform failures at peak season
‹ Overview
Market Context
Next: Thesis ›
01
The Thesis

Stability Is the Strategy

What's fundamentally true about this market — and what it means for the PMS

"You can't build trust on a platform operators can't rely on."

  • Demand is softer. Margins are tighter. Operators need tools that work — not tools that need managing.
  • Guest expectations have permanently moved. Marriott, Hilton, and Hyatt entered outdoor in 2024. The PMS is now the gap between what operators can deliver and what guests expect.
  • PE is consolidating the software layer. The window to build genuine operator trust is narrowing.
  • What becomes scarce: operator trust. What becomes commoditized: reservations, grid management, basic OTA connectivity.
  • Campspot's defensible moat is its data layer — but only if operators stay and use it.
‹ Market
01 · Thesis
Next: The Bet ›
02a
The Bet

Earn Back the Right to Build

The single commitment for the next 12–18 months

Why now
  • January 2026 platform migration is recent — operators still adjusting
  • NPS shows crashes, freezing, data loss at peak season18
  • Firsthand: Active error on the demo reservation grid (drag-and-drop), June 2026
  • Only 22% of operators have dynamic pricing enabled — despite award-winning parks at 67%2 — signals platform trust gap, not feature gap
  • Drag-and-drop released but still failing — education and stability problem, not an engineering gap
What has to be true
  • Engineering mandate to prioritize stability over new features
  • Tech debt is scope-able in sprints, not a multi-year rebuild
  • Release process (CAB or equivalent) exists for peak-season protection
  • NPS trackable at feature level, not just aggregate
  • AI direction piloted on new modules — not pursued as full rewrite13

Early signals it's working

  • Support tickets drop in crash / freeze / data loss categories
  • NPS comments shift from operational complaints to feature requests
  • Dynamic pricing and analytics adoption climbs
  • Reporting usage recovers after restoration

Early signals it's wrong

  • Tech debt is a rebuild, not sprints — timeline shifts to quarters
  • NPS flat despite fixes — signals pricing or trust problem, not product
  • Enterprise clients churn over missing features, not instability
On AI-native codebase AI-generated code has 2.7x higher vulnerability density vs. human-written. 75% of tech leaders expect moderate-to-severe technical debt from rapid AI development.13 Better near-term application: AI-assisted debugging via error log analysis. Pilot on new modules; don't rewrite the existing platform.
‹ Thesis
02a · The Bet
Next: Priorities ›
02b
The Bet — Continued

What This Unlocks

Primary bets first — secondary priorities follow once the foundation holds

Primary priorities
Primary
Platform stability Diagnose: scalability, integrations, or release cadence? Understand uptime nines. Scope tech debt before committing to timeline. Establish SRE function if absent. ROI: even 1% churn across 3,500 parks is material ARR.1,8
Primary
Restore reporting removed in migration Fixing a regression, not shipping a feature. Arrival/departure reports specifically cited.18 Operators should not lose functionality in a version upgrade.
Primary
Billing integrity Changing past invoices, incorrect due dates, inability to correct errors — this is a bug, not a UX complaint.18 Long-term stays are the most stable operator revenue segment.
Secondary priorities — unlocked once foundation holds
Secondary
Education & change management Parks using Analytics make 61.8% more revenue. Parks using dynamic pricing make 70.3% more.1 The product is there. The behavior isn't. Live webinars, seasonal staff onboarding, Campspot Certification.
Secondary
Dynamic pricing enhancement Current tool is rule-based. AI suggestions launched Nov 20253 but adoption is low (22% vs. 67% for top parks2). Gap: three-mode operator control — set-and-forget / approve each change / ignore with comparison graph showing suggested vs. actual. Removes the all-or-nothing friction keeping 78% of operators from enabling it.
Secondary
SMS / mass email communication Requires third-party add-ons today.4 Dynamic pricing is only as powerful as the marketing that fills the gaps it creates. AI-assisted message drafting is the natural next layer.18
Secondary
Payment processing flexibility Currently locked to Fiserv.4 Commercial decision above PM level — flag to leadership as churn driver.12
‹ The Bet
02b · Priorities
Next: Not List ›
03
The Not List

What We're Not Doing

Including things operators are actively asking for — and the reasoning behind each cut

Drag-and-drop grid (feature build) ★ asked for

  • Already released — confirmed on Campspot LinkedIn and product videos
  • Active error on the demo grid (June 2026) — that's a stability issue, not a reason to rebuild
  • Gap is education and adoption, not engineering
  • Open question: NPS date range vs. release date?

Full built-in CRM ★ asked for

  • Competes with Salesforce, HubSpot, tools operators already use
  • Better to integrate well than build badly
  • KOA built their entire PMS (K2) to own the loyalty relationship15,16 — that's the benchmark for doing it right
  • More targeted question: how many guests re-visit? May justify a lighter loyalty feature

Marketplace redesign / OTA conflict

  • Real operator frustration18 — but a business model decision, not a roadmap item
  • The dual PMS/OTA tension is not resolvable at the PM level
  • Flag to leadership as a retention risk

Pricing model changes ★ asked for

  • Campspot pricing is in line with the industry12
  • This is a GTM and commercial problem — the answer is showing more value, not reducing fees
  • Sales and CS conversation, not an engineering sprint
‹ Priorities
03 · Not List
Next: Evidence ›
04
Evidence

How the Feedback Shaped My Thinking

What I leaned on, what I discounted — and why

What I leaned on
  • NPS Theme 3 — Reliability18 — crashes at peak season, cross-segment. Weighted most heavily.
  • NPS Theme 9 — Reporting removed18 — operators lost functionality in a migration. Trust signal, not a feature request.
  • NPS Theme 6 — Invoicing18 — "nightmare" language is rare in NPS. Signals a bug, not a UX complaint.
  • NPS Theme 10 — Long-term stays18 — competitors winning here. Recurring revenue at risk.
  • NPS Theme 1 — Complexity18 — interpreted as progressive disclosure problem, not simplification problem.
  • CEO Webinar Jun 20266 — glamping +10.9% vs. −5.6% other parks. Cart abandonment and RevMgmt V2 listed as upcoming — relevant to sequencing.
  • Campspot 2025 Wrapped1 — 22% dynamic pricing adoption. 61.8% analytics uplift. 5% of bookings via marketplace.
  • Competitive landscape12,19 — Firefly and CampLife winning on long-term stays and price transparency.
  • Vertica thesis11 — "category dominance via M&A" shapes how to sequence stability vs. growth investment.
  • Firsthand (June 2026) — active error on Campspot demo grid. Current state, not history.
What I discounted — and why
  • NPS Theme 5 — Drag-and-drop18 — released since NPS collected. Gap is now education and adoption. Open question on NPS timing vs. release date.
  • NPS Theme 7 — Pricing18 — commercial decision. Campspot pricing is in line with industry.12
  • NPS Theme 11 — Marketplace18 — business model decision above PM level.
  • NPS Theme 2 — Support quality18 — leading indicator of product problems. Fix the platform; ticket volume drops.
  • Competitor comparison pages — vendor-authored; all claims cross-referenced against Campspot's own published pages where possible.

What I'm watching

  • How much of the complexity complaint is new platform (Jan 2026) vs. longstanding — need spike timing
  • Whether small vs. large operator fit is product architecture or segmentation strategy
  • OTA integration depth — are external bookings (Expedia, Airbnb) visible in the PMS grid, or siloed?
VoC theme map — all 12 themes
#ThemeWeightMaps toReasoning
1Complexity / learning curveHighProgressive disclosure / UX architectureNot "simplify everything" — the answer is tiered complexity; small parks feel overwhelmed, large parks need full power
2Support quality / responsivenessLow — discountedSymptom of product problemsFix the platform; ticket volume drops. Investing in headcount without fixing the product treats the symptom.
3Reliability / crashes at peak seasonHigh ★ PrimaryPlatform stability (Primary)Cross-segment; revenue-impacting; appears regardless of park size or type. Highest weight.
4Onboarding / training depthMediumEducation & change management (Secondary)Connects to adoption gap — parks using analytics and dynamic pricing dramatically outperform. Behavior change, not product gap.
5Drag-and-drop gridLow — reframedEducation & stability, not feature buildFeature released since NPS collected. Active error in demo (June 2026) confirms stability issue, not missing feature.
6Invoicing / billing accuracyHigh ★ PrimaryBilling integrity (Primary)"Nightmare" language is rare in NPS data. Signals a bug — changing past invoices, incorrect due dates — not UX friction.
7Pricing / cost of softwareLow — discountedGTM / commercial, not roadmapCampspot pricing is in line with the industry. Answer is demonstrating more value, not reducing fees.
8CRM / guest data managementMedium — watchingNot List (full CRM); lighter loyalty feature TBDBuilding a CRM competes with Salesforce/HubSpot. Better to integrate. Open question: repeat visitation rate data.
9Reporting removed in migrationHigh ★ PrimaryRestore reporting (Primary)Functionality lost in version upgrade = regression, not feature request. Arrival/departure reports specifically cited.
10Long-term / seasonal stay managementHighBilling integrity (Primary)Competitors (Firefly, CampLife) specifically winning defections here. Recurring revenue at risk.
11Marketplace / OTA conflictLow — discountedBusiness model, not PM roadmapDual PMS/OTA tension is a business strategy decision. Flag to leadership as retention risk.
12Small park vs. large park fitMedium — watchingUX architecture / segmentation (Secondary)Is this a product architecture issue or a customer segmentation strategy issue? Need to understand when complexity complaints spiked.

04 · Evidence
Next: Stakeholders ›
05
Bringing People Along

Where the Friction Lives

Sales, Support, Engineering, and a major operator will each react differently to this plan

Sales

  • Friction: sells on roadmap — stability investment slows feature announcements
  • Concern: short-term pipeline pressure; operators asking for features that aren't coming
  • Bridge: platform reliability IS a sales story — connect uptime to renewal rates and NRR
  • Give them: "operators on stable platforms churn less" as a closing argument, not a concession

Support

  • Friction: headcount needs before product is fixed
  • Likely your biggest internal ally — they see every crash ticket and invoicing complaint
  • Bring them in early as a data source; their ground-level patterns don't always make it into NPS summaries
  • Bridge: stability investment directly reduces their ticket volume — this is their win too

Engineering

  • Friction: tech debt scope unknown; AI-native direction creates competing priorities
  • Come with questions, not answers: uptime nines, CAB process, debt backlog shape, SRE capacity
  • AI reframe: error log debugging via AI is proven, low-risk, immediately valuable — pilot on new modules, don't rewrite existing platform
  • Risk: if debt is a rebuild not sprints, timeline shifts from months to quarters

Major Operator

  • Friction: feedback acknowledged but not acted on; new features promised before foundation is fixed
  • Show up with a concrete timeline on reporting restoration — not just acknowledgment
  • Operators talk to each other; winning one back loudly is worth more than a quiet fix
  • Risk: they may ask for new features before stability work is complete — sequencing conversation required
‹ Evidence

Next: Competitors ›
A1
Archived Reference · Competitive Landscape

Campspot vs. the Field

All figures cross-referenced across multiple sources. Treat competitor-cited pricing as directional.12

PMS
Property Management System — daily operations software: reservations, billing, check-in, staff tasks.
OTA
Online Travel Agency — marketplace where campers discover and book parks (e.g. Campspot.com).
Grid Optimizer
Algorithm that shuffles bookings to reduce gaps and maximize occupancy. Campspot pioneered this.
Dynamic Pricing
Auto-adjusting rates based on occupancy, time of year, and booking window.17
Channel Manager
Tool syncing availability across multiple OTAs to prevent double-bookings.
RevPAS
Revenue Per Available Site — the campground equivalent of hotel RevPAR.
Dimension 🔶 Campspot CampLife ResNexus Firefly RoverPass NewBook (Storable)

PE-backed (Vertica)		 ~600–900 parks
ARVC Supplier of Year '19–'20		 Hundreds; growing fast
Founded 2004		 Smaller, fast-growing
Sister: ReserveAmerica		 100s of thousands marketplace visitors 55,000+ users
Acquired by Storable, May 2024
Best Fit Mid-to-large parks, portfolios, marketplace-dependent All sizes; repeat guests; complex pricing; camp stores Glamping resorts; B&Bs; luxury outdoor Private campgrounds + RV parks; municipalities; direct-booking focus Small-to-mid RV parks; operators new to software Larger operations; multi-property groups; automation-first
PE Ownership Vertica Independent Independent Aspira / ReserveAmerica Independent Storable / EQT
Pricing Model ~$3/booking + ~3% commission + 10% marketplace fee

No marketplace fee; transparent		 Per-booking fee; $3.50/mo base Essentials $200–250/mo + $3.50/booking

Long-term / Seasonal Weak — no open-ended stays; forced end date; invoicing pain Strong — recurring invoicing, remote metering Moderate Strong — long-term reservations built-in Strong — long-term stay management included Moderate — tier-dependent
Campspot SWOT

Strengths

  • Grid optimizer — invented the category; deep operator muscle memory
  • Dynamic pricing engine; best-in-class when used5
  • Consumer marketplace — 73.4% of marketplace bookers are new-to-park1
  • All-in-one: reservations, POS, utilities, analytics
  • 3.7M reservations / 54M site nights of proprietary data1
  • PE backing (Vertica) = capital for product investment11

Weaknesses

  • Peak-season crashes, freezing, data loss18
  • Billing bugs — past invoices changing, incorrect due dates18
  • Reporting removed in Jan 2026 migration18
  • Only 22% dynamic pricing adoption2 — education and trust gap
  • Locked to Fiserv — no processor flexibility4
  • OTA conflict: Campspot captures guest data from marketplace bookings

Opportunities

  • Glamping +10.9% YoY — weather-independent, experience-first6
  • AI-assisted debugging and error log analysis
  • Three-mode dynamic pricing control — closes adoption gap
  • Cross-park loyalty program (direct bookings only — OTA bookings structurally excluded)
  • Enterprise/multi-park groups scaling — Campspot Voyager
  • SMS/email + AI message drafting to activate dynamic pricing

Threats

  • Firefly + CampLife winning defections on long-term stays and transparency12,19
  • Marriott, Hilton, Hyatt entering outdoor — guest expectations raised9,10
  • PE consolidation may commoditize core features12
  • KOA built own PMS (K2) to own loyalty — largest operator not a client15,16
  • Operator perception: "Campspot is for big campgrounds"18
  • Airbnb 2025 policy tightening — guest identity locked; loyalty play structurally blocked for OTA bookings

A2
Archived Reference · Methodology

Assumptions, Sources & Caveats

What this presentation is built on — and what to watch out for

Primary Campspot Sources

  • 2025 Wrapped1 — 3.7M reservations, 54M site nights, analytics/dynamic pricing uplifts, marketplace stats. First-party data.
  • Pricing page4 — confirms guest-paid fee model, no setup fees, third-party tool requirements for SMS/email
  • Growth & Revenue page5 — confirms dynamic pricing as named product feature
  • Dynamic pricing knowledge base17 — rule-based mechanics, occupancy thresholds, booking window
  • AI pricing launch (Nov 14, 2025)3 — predictive suggestions feature live date
  • NPS / VoC Candidate Packet18 — ~3,750 responses, ~560 written comments, overall NPS +28. Dissatisfied operators more likely to write — comments skew negative.
  • CEO Webinar (Jun 18, 2026)6 — provided as PDF; Campspot's own market data
  • Demo environment (Jun 2026) — active error on drag-and-drop reservation grid. Not publicly sourced — firsthand.

Key Caveats

  • Competitor pricing is directional only — sourced from vendor comparison pages with inherent bias. Cross-referenced against Campspot's own pages.
  • AI code vulnerability stats (2.7x, 75%, 67%)13 — SQ Magazine aggregating "Second Talent." Original primary source not independently verified. Present as directional industry research.
  • Industry benchmarks7,8,14 — public estimates used alongside Campspot's own data throughout.
  • ROI connections — logic-based estimates using public benchmarks and Campspot's own published figures.
  • Dynamic pricing adoption stat (22% / 67%)2 — from August 2024 webinar. May have changed since.
  • AI use disclosure — Built with Claude (Anthropic, claude-sonnet-4-6). Point of view, thesis, cuts, and overrides are the candidate's own.

  • 2 Campspot — Dynamic Pricing Strategy Webinar
